title: 24/7: Late Capitalism and the Ends of Sleep
author: Jonathan Crary
tagline: A fierce polemic against the non-stop demands of contemporary life.
description: Crary argues that contemporary capitalism is waging a war on sleep — the last human activity that cannot be colonised by market forces. In a 24/7 world of perpetual connectivity, non-stop consumption, and round-the-clock labour, sleep represents a scandalous interruption: an interval of time that cannot be productised. The book traces how illumination, screens, and the erosion of day-night boundaries have reshaped human experience, drawing on art, neuroscience, and critical theory. It's a short, blazing polemic — less a policy argument than a philosophical defence of the irreducible human need for rest and withdrawal.

themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: Crary reveals the hidden war that capitalism wages on human consciousness — colonising attention, eroding the boundary between day and night, and attacking sleep as an unproductive interruption. The violence is invisible precisely because it has become the texture of daily life.
themeNotes.The Body as Battleground: Sleep is the body's refusal to be productive. Crary frames the biological need for rest as a site of resistance — the last frontier where the body asserts its own rhythms against the demands of the market.

title: 5:2 Veggie & Vegan
author: Kate Harrison
tagline: Plant-based recipes for the 5:2 intermittent fasting diet.
description: Vegetarian and vegan recipes designed around the 5:2 eating pattern, combining fasting-friendly calorie counts with genuinely appealing food.

title: #Accelerate
author: Various
tagline: The accelerationist reader — essays on whether to speed up or slow down the processes of capitalism.
description: A collection spanning Marx, Deleuze, Nick Land, and contemporary accelerationists, debating whether the way beyond capitalism is to resist its speed or to push it further and faster until it transforms into something else.

title: Adventures of Ideas
author: Alfred North Whitehead
tagline: Whitehead traces how ideas — freedom, beauty, truth — have shaped civilisation across millennia.
description: Process philosopher Whitehead examines how abstract ideas have material consequences, tracing the history of concepts like freedom, peace, and beauty from ancient Greece to the modern world. Philosophy as cultural history.

title: Aesthetic Theory
author: Theodor W. Adorno
tagline: Adorno's unfinished masterwork on art's relationship to truth, society, and suffering.
description: Adorno argues that authentic art resists the administered world not through political content but through its form — its refusal to be easily consumed. Art's truth content lies in its negativity, in what it refuses to reconcile. Published posthumously and deliberately fragmentary.

themeNotes.Seeing and Power: Adorno argues that art's power lies not in what it shows but in what it refuses to show — its resistance to the culture industry's demand that everything be made visible, consumable, and profitable.

title: Against Nature
author: J.K. Huysmans
tagline: Des Esseintes retreats from society into an aesthetic cocoon of art, perfume, and artificial flowers.
description: The ultimate novel of decadence. Des Esseintes, a jaded aristocrat, withdraws from the world and constructs an entirely artificial environment — gem-encrusted tortoise, orchestras of liqueurs, rooms designed for each hour of the day. A novel that influenced Wilde, Proust, and the entire Symbolist movement.

title: Alice Neel
author: Various
tagline: A monograph on the American painter who spent decades painting the people of New York — radically honest, never flattering.
description: Neel painted her neighbours in Spanish Harlem, her communist friends, pregnant nudes, and dying men with an unflinching honesty that was decades ahead of its time. This book surveys her life's work.

title: Animal Joy
author: Nuar Alsadir
tagline: A psychoanalyst and poet explores spontaneous expression — laughter, crying, blushing — and why we suppress it.
description: Alsadir investigates the involuntary expressions the body makes — the laugh, the blush, the cry — as moments when something authentic breaks through the social self. Drawing on psychoanalysis, poetry, and clown school, she asks what we lose when we learn to suppress these eruptions.

title: Antifragile
author: Nassim Nicholas Taleb
tagline: Taleb argues that some things don't just survive shocks — they get stronger from them.
description: Beyond resilience (which merely survives) lies antifragility — the property of systems that improve under stress. Taleb applies this idea to economics, health, politics, and personal life, arguing that we should design for volatility rather than trying to eliminate it.

themeNotes.Ruins and Resilience: Taleb goes beyond resilience to argue that the best systems actually benefit from disorder. Not just surviving the ruins but being strengthened by them — antifragility as a principle of life.

title: The Argonauts
author: Maggie Nelson
tagline: A genre-breaking memoir about desire, gender, motherhood, and theory.
description: Nelson writes about her relationship with the artist Harry Dodge — who is transitioning — while she is pregnant, weaving in Barthes, Wittgenstein, Judith Butler, and Eve Kosofsky Sedgwick. It's a book about how language both creates and fails to capture the people we love.

themeNotes.The Body as Battleground: Two bodies in transformation — one through pregnancy, one through transition. Nelson shows that the body is never fixed but always becoming, and that love is the willingness to accompany that becoming.

title: Ariel
author: Sylvia Plath
tagline: Plath's devastating final collection — poetry as absolute intensity.
description: Written in the final months of Plath's life, these poems blaze with a ferocity and control that stunned the literary world when they were published posthumously in 1965. "Lady Lazarus," "Daddy," "Ariel," "Edge" — each poem is an act of transformation, turning personal suffering into art of terrifying beauty. Plath's voice in these poems is unlike anything that came before: precise, furious, darkly comic, and absolutely without self-pity. The collection has been endlessly debated — is it confessional? feminist? mythic? — but its power is beyond argument. These are poems that change the temperature of the room they're read in.

themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: "Lady Lazarus" rises from the dead; "Daddy" excavates the buried father. Plath's poems are eruptions — the controlled surface of language splitting open to reveal fury, pain, and a terrifying clarity that was always there, underneath.
themeNotes.The Body as Battleground: Plath writes from inside the body with unprecedented intensity — the body burned, resurrected, cut open, transformed. In Ariel, the female body is simultaneously a prison and the raw material for the most powerful art of its era.

title: Art Beyond Representation
author: Barbara Bolt
tagline: Bolt rethinks the relationship between art practice and theory — making as a form of thinking.
description: Drawing on Heidegger and Deleuze, Bolt argues that art doesn't represent the world — it performs it. The studio is not a place where ideas are illustrated but where new realities are produced through material engagement.

title: Art as Far as the Eye Can See
author: Paul Virilio
tagline: Virilio on the crisis of contemporary art in an age of total visibility.
description: Virilio argues that when everything can be seen, shown, and transmitted instantly, art loses its capacity to reveal. The overexposure of images produces not illumination but blindness.

title: The Art of Participation: 1950 to Now
author: Various
tagline: The SFMOMA exhibition catalogue on participatory and socially engaged art.
description: A survey of art that requires the viewer's participation — from Fluxus happenings to relational aesthetics to contemporary social practice, asking what happens when the audience becomes the artist.

title: The Artist's Way
author: Julia Cameron
tagline: Cameron's twelve-week course in creative recovery — morning pages, artist dates, and learning to let go.
description: The most widely used creativity course in the world. Cameron's central tools — morning pages (three pages of longhand stream-of-consciousness writing every day) and the artist date (a solo outing to fill the creative well) — have helped millions of blocked creatives start making work again.

title: As Bill Sees It
author: Alcoholics Anonymous
tagline: Daily reflections from AA co-founder Bill Wilson — short passages on humility, gratitude, and the spiritual life.
description: A collection of brief excerpts from Bill Wilson's letters and writings, organised for daily reading. Each entry addresses a theme of recovery: acceptance, humility, service, and the ongoing practice of spiritual surrender.

title: Atlas of Brutalist Architecture
author: Phaidon
tagline: A monumental survey of over 850 Brutalist buildings across the globe.
description: This encyclopaedic volume catalogues more than 850 Brutalist buildings in 102 countries — from the Barbican in London to the National Theatre of Ghana, from Le Corbusier's Unité d'Habitation to forgotten masterpieces in the former Soviet Union. Each entry includes photographs, plans, and contextual essays explaining the building's place in the Brutalist movement. The atlas argues for Brutalism not as a cold, inhuman style but as an idealistic architecture of social ambition — buildings designed to house communities, educate nations, and express the aspirations of the post-war world. An essential reference and a visual feast.

themeNotes.Ruins and Resilience: Brutalism was born from the rubble of World War II — an architecture of reconstruction, built in concrete because concrete was cheap and ambition was not. These buildings are monuments to the belief that you could build a better society from the ruins of the old one.
themeNotes.Place and Belonging: Every Brutalist building was designed to shape how people live together — housing estates, universities, civic centres. The atlas documents architecture as an act of community-making, where the built environment embodies a society's aspirations for collective belonging.

title: Averno
author: Louise Glück
tagline: Glück reimagines the myth of Persephone — descent, loss, and the landscape of the underworld.
description: Named after the volcanic lake the Romans believed was the entrance to the underworld, this collection retells the Persephone myth as a meditation on death, winter, marriage, and the daughter's separation from the mother. Nobel Prize-winning poetry at its most austere and powerful.

themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: Averno — the entrance to the underworld. Glück's poems descend into the earth, into myth, into the space between the living and the dead. The entire collection is a journey downward.

title: Being and Event
author: Alain Badiou
tagline: Badiou's magnum opus — a radical rethinking of ontology through set theory.
description: Badiou's central claim is extraordinary: that mathematics (specifically, Zermelo-Fraenkel set theory) is ontology — the science of being as such. From this foundation, he builds a philosophical system that accounts for how genuine novelty enters the world through what he calls "events" — unpredictable ruptures (a revolution, a scientific breakthrough, a love encounter, an artistic innovation) that cannot be explained by the existing order. The subject, for Badiou, is not a given but emerges through fidelity to such events. Dense, demanding, and exhilarating, Being and Event is one of the most ambitious works of philosophy since Heidegger.

themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: Badiou argues that the most important things — revolutions, love, art — erupt from beneath the surface of "what is." The event is precisely what the existing order cannot predict or contain. Truth is what lies beneath being.

title: Badiou: A Subject to Truth
author: Peter Hallward
tagline: The most comprehensive English-language introduction to Badiou's philosophical system.
description: Hallward guides the reader through Being and Event, the theory of the subject, and Badiou's writings on politics, art, love, and science, providing the context and clarity that Badiou's own prose sometimes withholds.

title: Jean Baudrillard: Art and Artefice
author: Nicholas Zurbrugg
tagline: Zurbrugg interviews Baudrillard on art, simulation, and the fate of reality in the age of the hyperreal.
description: Extended conversations in which Baudrillard discusses his relationship to contemporary art, the difference between simulation and representation, and why he believes reality has been replaced by its own image.

title: The Beautiful Risk of Education
author: Gert Biesta
tagline: Biesta argues that real education is risky — it cannot be controlled, measured, or guaranteed.
description: Against the culture of educational measurement and accountability, Biesta argues that the most important things education does — creating subjects, enabling freedom, fostering responsibility — are inherently unpredictable and cannot be reduced to outcomes.

title: Becoming Animal
author: David Abram
tagline: Abram argues for a return to sensory experience — recovering our animal participation in the more-than-human world.
description: Drawing on phenomenology and indigenous ways of knowing, Abram invites the reader to rediscover the intelligence of the body and its kinship with the animate earth. Shadows, sounds, wind, and rain become doorways to a deeper engagement with the living world.

title: Becoming Supernatural
author: Dr. Joe Dispenza
tagline: Dispenza on using meditation, breath, and intention to access states of consciousness beyond the ordinary.
description: Combining neuroscience with spiritual practice, Dispenza presents techniques for transcending the body and the familiar self — arguing that sustained meditation can produce measurable changes in brain function and physical health.

title: Being and Time
author: Martin Heidegger
tagline: Heidegger's foundational inquiry into the meaning of Being — the book that reshaped continental philosophy.
description: Heidegger asks the question that philosophy had forgotten: what does it mean to be? Through his analysis of Dasein — human existence as "being-there" — he reveals the structures of everyday life: care, anxiety, being-toward-death, and the call of conscience. Unfinished, difficult, and unavoidable.

themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: Heidegger argues that our everyday existence conceals the question of Being beneath a surface of routine and distraction. Anxiety — Angst — is what breaks through, revealing the nothing that underlies everything.

title: Bells and Whistles
author: Graham Harman
tagline: Harman's more accessible essays — on speculative realism, aesthetics, and the philosophical value of objects.
description: A collection of shorter pieces that introduce Harman's philosophical project to a wider audience, applying object-oriented thinking to art, architecture, and everyday experience.

title: Beloved
author: Toni Morrison
tagline: A ghost story and searing exploration of slavery's legacy — Morrison's most powerful work.
description: Sethe, a formerly enslaved woman living in Cincinnati after the Civil War, is haunted — literally — by the ghost of her dead daughter. When a young woman called Beloved appears on her doorstep, the past that Sethe has tried to bury begins to resurface: the horrors of Sweet Home plantation, her desperate escape, and the unthinkable act she committed to save her children from slavery. Morrison creates a narrative that moves like memory itself — fragmented, circling, and devastatingly precise. Beloved is not just a novel about slavery but about what slavery does to the inner life — to love, to motherhood, to the very possibility of selfhood.

themeNotes.The Politics of Otherness: Slavery is the ultimate othering — the legal reduction of a person to property. Morrison shows how this dehumanisation persists long after emancipation, shaping how Sethe sees herself, her children, and her right to love.
themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: The ghost of Beloved is the past that refuses to stay buried. Morrison makes literal what is psychologically true: that trauma lives in the house, in the body, in the very air — and that it will manifest until it is faced.
themeNotes.The Body as Battleground: Sethe's body bears the scars of slavery — literally, a tree of welts on her back. Her desperate act of violence is a mother's attempt to keep her children's bodies free. The novel insists that slavery was, above all, a crime against the body.

title: Berlin
author: Rory MacLean
tagline: A vivid portrait of Berlin through five centuries of dreamers and tyrants.
description: MacLean tells the story of Berlin through the lives of its inhabitants across five hundred years — from the Great Elector to Marlene Dietrich, from Albert Speer to David Bowie. Each chapter focuses on a historical figure who embodied the city's restless capacity for reinvention: the Huguenot refugees who made it cosmopolitan, the artists who made it bohemian, the Nazis who tried to make it a world capital, and the ravers who reclaimed it after the Wall came down. It's a book about a city that has been destroyed and rebuilt so many times that transformation is its defining characteristic.

themeNotes.Ruins and Resilience: Berlin is the city of ruins and resilience — bombed flat, divided by a wall, reunified, gentrified. MacLean shows that Berlin's identity is not any single incarnation but the pattern of destruction and reinvention itself.
themeNotes.Place and Belonging: From Huguenot refugees to Turkish guest workers to post-Wall artists, Berlin has always been shaped by people who arrived from elsewhere. MacLean argues that the city's genius lies in its capacity to absorb newcomers and be transformed by them.

title: Bhagavad Gita
author: Swami Sivananda (trans.)
tagline: The sacred Hindu dialogue between Arjuna and Krishna on duty, action, and the nature of the self.
description: On the battlefield of Kurukshetra, the warrior Arjuna falters. Krishna — his charioteer and an incarnation of God — reveals the nature of the self, the paths of action and devotion, and the secret of acting without attachment to results. The most influential text in Hindu philosophy.

title: Bill Viola: Writings 1973–1994
author: Bill Viola
tagline: The video artist's journals, notebooks, and essays — on perception, time, water, and the inner life of images.
description: Viola's writings reveal the philosophical depth behind his monumental video installations. He writes about medieval mystics, about the relationship between perception and consciousness, and about how technology can create spaces for contemplation.

title: Black Mountain Poems
author: Various
tagline: Poetry from the Black Mountain school — Olson, Creeley, Duncan — process, breath, and the open field.
description: An anthology of the poets associated with Black Mountain College, who revolutionised American poetry by treating the page as an open field and the poem as an act of discovery rather than a finished object.

title: Mind-Forg'd Manacles: William Blake and Slavery
author: Various
tagline: Essays on Blake's engagement with the abolitionist movement and his radical visual politics.
description: A collection exploring how Blake's poetry and art responded to the slave trade — his illustrations for abolitionist publications, the imagery of chains and liberation in his prophetic books, and his broader critique of empire.

title: Blue Nights
author: Joan Didion
tagline: Didion on the death of her daughter Quintana — ageing, mortality, and the failure of memory.
description: Written after the death of her daughter Quintana Roo, Blue Nights is Didion's meditation on parenthood, ageing, and the terrifying fragility of life. Where The Year of Magical Thinking was about grief, this is about fear — the fear that comes with growing old and losing the ability to protect those you love.

themeNotes.Ruins and Resilience: After the ruin of her husband's death (documented in Magical Thinking), Didion faces another: her daughter's death. Blue Nights is about what is left when resilience itself begins to fail — the body ageing, memory fading.
themeNotes.The Body as Battleground: Didion writes about ageing as a physical experience — the body's betrayals, the loss of confidence in her own strength. The book is as much about embodied vulnerability as it is about grief.

title: The Book of Life
author: Jiddu Krishnamurti
tagline: Daily meditations from Krishnamurti — on freedom, fear, love, and the nature of thought.
description: Three hundred and sixty-five passages drawn from Krishnamurti's talks and writings, organised for daily contemplation. Each entry addresses the fundamental questions of human existence with Krishnamurti's characteristic directness.

title: The Book You Wish Your Parents Had Read
author: Philippa Perry
tagline: Perry on how to break the cycle of inherited parenting patterns — with warmth, honesty, and no guilt trips.
description: A psychotherapist explains how our own childhood experiences shape how we parent, and how to do better without beating ourselves up about it. Practical, compassionate, and grounded in attachment theory.

title: Braiding Sweetgrass
author: Robin Wall Kimmerer
tagline: Indigenous wisdom and scientific knowledge woven together in a meditation on the land.
description: Kimmerer, a professor of ecology and a member of the Citizen Potawatomi Nation, weaves together indigenous knowledge and Western science to argue for a new relationship with the natural world — one based on gratitude, reciprocity, and responsibility rather than extraction. She writes about sweetgrass and wild strawberries, about the gift economy of indigenous cultures, about teaching her students to see plants as teachers rather than objects. Each essay is a meditation on what it means to live in a world where everything — plants, animals, water, soil — is alive and deserving of respect. It's a book that has quietly become one of the most important environmental texts of the century.

themeNotes.Ruins and Resilience: Kimmerer writes from the ruins of colonisation — the near-destruction of indigenous languages, knowledge systems, and land. Yet Braiding Sweetgrass is not a book about loss but about what survives: the sweetgrass still grows, the stories are still told, and reciprocity with the land remains possible.

title: Breathing Under Water
author: Richard Rohr
tagline: Rohr reads the twelve steps of recovery as a universal spiritual programme — not just for addicts but for anyone trapped in compulsive patterns.
description: Rohr argues that the twelve-step programme of Alcoholics Anonymous is one of the most profound spiritual frameworks of the modern era, applicable far beyond addiction. Each step maps onto a movement in the spiritual life: admitting powerlessness, surrendering to something greater, making amends, and practising ongoing self-examination.

themeNotes.Ruins and Resilience: Rohr frames recovery as a universal pattern of resilience — the destruction of the false self as the precondition for genuine spiritual life. You have to hit bottom before you can rise.

title: A Brief History of Curating
author: Hans Ulrich Obrist
tagline: Obrist interviews the pioneers of contemporary exhibition-making.
description: Conversations with the curators who invented the contemporary art exhibition as we know it — Harald Szeemann, Pontus Hultén, Walter Hopps, Lucy Lippard, and others. A history of how art comes to be shown, seen, and understood.

title: A Guide to the Buddhist Path
author: Sangharakshita
tagline: A comprehensive introduction to Buddhist thought and practice by the founder of the Triratna Buddhist Community.
description: Sangharakshita presents the entire Buddhist path — from the historical Buddha through the development of the Mahayana to contemporary practice — as a living tradition of ethical and spiritual transformation.

title: Selected Poetry
author: Lord Byron
tagline: Byron's essential poems — from the romantic exuberance of Childe Harold to the satirical brilliance of Don Juan.
description: The poet who was "mad, bad, and dangerous to know" at his best: the passionate, witty, and scandalous verse that made him the most famous poet in Europe.

title: Capital (Volume 1)
author: Karl Marx
tagline: Marx's analysis of the commodity form, surplus value, and the inner workings of capitalism.
description: Beginning with the commodity — "an object outside us, a thing that by its properties satisfies human wants" — Marx traces how capitalism transforms human labour into abstract value, how surplus is extracted, and how the system reproduces itself. The foundational text of political economy.

title: Chaosmosis
author: Félix Guattari
tagline: Guattari's late work on the production of subjectivity — how selves are made by aesthetic, social, and machinic processes.
description: Guattari argues that subjectivity is not given but produced — by media, institutions, language, and the unconscious. He proposes an "ecosophy" that integrates aesthetic creation with political and environmental transformation.

title: Cinema 1: The Movement-Image
author: Gilles Deleuze
tagline: Deleuze creates a new philosophy of cinema through Bergson, Peirce, and the history of film.
description: Deleuze argues that cinema doesn't represent movement — it is movement-image and time-image. Drawing on Bergson's philosophy, he creates a taxonomy of cinematic signs through close readings of Hitchcock, Eisenstein, Hawks, and the Italian Neorealists.

title: Citizen: An American Lyric
author: Claudia Rankine
tagline: Poetry, essay, and image documenting the everyday experience of racism.
description: Citizen defies classification. It's a book of poetry that reads like an essay, structured around a series of "microaggressions" — moments of casual racism experienced by the speaker and other Black Americans in everyday life. A friend calls you by the wrong name. A therapist opens the door and says, "I was expecting someone else." A tennis player is penalised for an outburst that would go unremarked in a white player. Rankine uses the second person throughout — "you" — implicating the reader in every encounter. Interspersed are visual artworks, meditations on Serena Williams and Trayvon Martin, and longer prose pieces. It sold over a million copies and changed what contemporary poetry could do.

themeNotes.The Politics of Otherness: Rankine documents othering as it actually happens — in real time, in small moments, in the gap between what was said and what was meant. The "you" of the text makes the reader inhabit the experience of being other, if only for 169 pages.
themeNotes.Seeing and Power: By making racial microaggressions visible — naming them, cataloguing them, refusing to let them pass — Rankine turns seeing into a political act. The book forces you to notice what you've been trained not to see.
themeNotes.The Body as Battleground: Rankine documents what it means to move through the world in a Black body — the hypervisibility, the surveillance, the exhaustion of being constantly perceived as a threat. The body in Citizen is never just a body; it's always a political surface.

title: A Clinical Introduction to Lacanian Psychoanalysis
author: Bruce Fink
tagline: Fink on how Lacanian theory translates into clinical practice — diagnosis, transference, and the direction of treatment.
description: A practical companion to The Lacanian Subject, showing how Lacan's concepts of neurosis, psychosis, and perversion inform actual analytic work. Written for clinicians but illuminating for anyone interested in how the unconscious manifests in symptoms.

title: A Concise History of Modern Painting
author: Herbert Read
tagline: Read's classic survey of modern art from Cézanne to Abstract Expressionism.
description: First published in 1959, Read's history traces the development of modern painting through Impressionism, Fauvism, Cubism, Expressionism, Surrealism, and abstraction — written with a poet's eye for the significance of each movement.

title: The Conscious Parent
author: Shefali Tsabary
tagline: Tsabary argues that parenting is primarily a spiritual practice — children are our greatest teachers.
description: Drawing on Eastern philosophy and Western psychology, Tsabary proposes that conscious parenting means using the parent-child relationship as a vehicle for the parent's own growth — letting go of control, meeting the child as they are, and recognising your own projections.

title: The Constance Spry Cookery Book
author: Constance Spry & Rosemary Hume
tagline: The definitive mid-century British cookery book — encyclopaedic and enduringly practical.
description: First published in 1956, this mammoth volume was the bible of post-war British cooking. Constance Spry, the legendary flower arranger and domestic arts educator, teamed with Rosemary Hume of the Cordon Bleu school to produce a comprehensive guide covering everything from basic stocks to elaborate dinner party menus. The recipes are classical — soufflés, terrines, roasts, sauces — but the voice is surprisingly modern: opinionated, practical, and often very funny. Spry invented Coronation Chicken for the Queen's coronation in 1953, and the recipe is here alongside hundreds of others that defined an era of British entertaining.

title: The Continental Aesthetics Reader
author: Clive Cazeaux (ed.)
tagline: The essential anthology of continental philosophy of art — from Kant and Hegel through Heidegger, Adorno, and Derrida.
description: A comprehensive reader covering the major figures and movements in continental aesthetics: German Idealism, phenomenology, hermeneutics, critical theory, structuralism, post-structuralism, and contemporary debates.

title: Cook, Eat, Repeat
author: Nigella Lawson
tagline: Nigella on the rhythms of cooking — recipes interwoven with essays on food, memory, and obsession.
description: Part cookbook, part food memoir, Nigella weaves recipes with essays on the pleasures and compulsions of cooking. From the joy of brown food to the comfort of repetition, it's an intimate portrait of a life lived in the kitchen.

title: The Cooking of Vienna's Empire
author: Joseph Wechsberg (Time-Life)
tagline: A culinary tour through the Austro-Hungarian Empire, from pastries to goulash.
description: Part of the classic Time-Life Foods of the World series, this volume explores the kitchens of the old Austro-Hungarian Empire — from the coffeehouses of Vienna to the farmhouses of Hungary, Bohemia, and the Balkans. Joseph Wechsberg, a Czech-born writer and gastronome, guides the reader through Sachertorte, Wiener Schnitzel, Hungarian goulash, Czech dumplings, and the elaborate pastry traditions that made Viennese baking legendary. Richly illustrated and written with warmth and authority, it captures a vanished culinary world where empires were held together partly by what people ate.

title: The Covenant of Water
author: Abraham Verghese
tagline: Three generations of a Kerala family bound by a mysterious curse — someone in each generation dies by drowning.
description: Spanning from 1900 to 1977, Verghese's epic follows a family in Kerala through colonialism, independence, and social upheaval, all shadowed by an inherited condition that means at least one member of each generation will drown. Medicine, love, and the weight of family history intertwine.

title: Critique of Judgement
author: Immanuel Kant
tagline: Kant's third critique — on beauty, the sublime, and the purposiveness of nature.
description: Kant asks how we can judge something beautiful without relying on concepts, and how nature can appear purposeful without a designer. The Critique of Judgement bridges his earlier works on knowledge and morality, and laid the groundwork for modern aesthetics.

themeNotes.Seeing and Power: Kant's aesthetics reveal that judgements of beauty are never purely personal — they claim universal assent. The politics of taste, of who gets to define the beautiful, begins here.

title: The Cultural Turn
author: Fredric Jameson
tagline: Jameson's selected writings on postmodernity — architecture, film, and the end of art.
description: Essays from the 1980s and 1990s in which Jameson applies his Marxist cultural theory to the Bonaventure Hotel, David Lynch, and the crisis of historicity in the postmodern era.

title: The Culture Industry
author: Theodor W. Adorno
tagline: Adorno's essays on how mass culture manufactures conformity disguised as choice.
description: The culture industry produces the illusion of individuality while standardising everything. These essays — on jazz, television, astrology, and free time — argue that entertainment is not harmless pleasure but a mechanism of social control.

title: Dark Nights of the Soul
author: Thomas Moore
tagline: Moore reclaims the "dark night" from pathology — depression and despair as invitations to deeper life.
description: Drawing on the Christian mystical tradition of St. John of the Cross, Moore argues that periods of darkness, depression, and meaninglessness are not illnesses to be medicated but necessary passages in the soul's development. The dark night is not a breakdown but a breakthrough.

themeNotes.Ruins and Resilience: Moore reframes depression and spiritual crisis as forms of necessary ruin — the dark night must be endured, not avoided, because what emerges on the other side is a deeper, more authentic self.

title: Democracy and Education
author: John Dewey
tagline: Dewey's foundational argument that education and democracy are inseparable — learning as shared inquiry.
description: Dewey argues that education is not preparation for life but life itself — a continuous process of growth through shared experience. Democracy, for Dewey, is not merely a political system but a mode of associated living that education either supports or undermines.

title: Depression: A Very Short Introduction
author: Various (Oxford)
tagline: A concise overview of depression — its causes, manifestations, and treatments.
description: An accessible introduction to what is known about depression from clinical, psychological, and neurobiological perspectives, covering diagnosis, therapy, medication, and the ongoing debate about what depression actually is.

title: Design for Social Innovation
author: Ezio Manzini
tagline: Manzini argues that design can catalyse grassroots social change — when everyone designs, everything changes.
description: A manifesto for design as a tool of social transformation, drawing on case studies of community-led initiatives — from urban food networks to co-housing — where design thinking has enabled new forms of collaborative living.

title: Dishoom: From Bombay with Love
author: Shamil Thakrar, Kavi Thakrar & Naved Nasir
tagline: The story and recipes behind the beloved Bombay café, woven with tales of old Irani cafés.
description: Dishoom is part cookbook, part love letter to the Irani cafés of Bombay — those once-ubiquitous gathering places where chai was bottomless and conversation flowed freely. The Thakrar brothers trace the history of these cafés, many now vanished, and share the recipes that made their London restaurants a phenomenon: the legendary black daal (simmered for 24 hours), the bacon naan roll, the lamb biryani, the chicken ruby. Between the recipes are stories of old Bombay — the mill workers, the film stars, the characters who gave each café its personality. Beautifully photographed and deeply personal.

title: Don't Let Me Be Lonely
author: Claudia Rankine
tagline: A lyric meditation on depression, death, and media saturation.
description: Subtitled "An American Lyric" — the same designation Rankine would later give to Citizen — this earlier work is a fragmented meditation on loneliness, death, and the numbing effect of mass media. Rankine watches CNN coverage of September 11, considers the pharmaceutical industry's colonisation of sadness, visits a dying friend, and reflects on what it means to live in a country that is both her home and a source of constant alienation. The book alternates between prose poetry and reproductions of television static, creating a work that feels like channel-surfing through a culture in crisis. Less widely known than Citizen but equally essential.

themeNotes.The Politics of Otherness: Rankine describes the loneliness of belonging to a country that does not fully recognise you. The alienation here is quieter than in Citizen but no less devastating — a slow erosion of selfhood by a culture that renders you invisible.

title: The Dreamer's Dictionary
author: Various
tagline: A reference guide to dream symbols and their meanings.
description: An A–Z of dream imagery — from animals to water to flying — offering interpretations drawn from psychoanalytic, Jungian, and folk traditions.

title: Dyes from Plants
author: Various
tagline: A practical guide to extracting and using natural dyes from common plants.
description: Recipes and techniques for dyeing wool, silk, and cotton using plants — from the familiar (blackberries, nettles) to the more exotic (indigo, cochineal). A companion for anyone interested in colour, craft, and the chemistry of nature.

title: East
author: Meera Sodha
tagline: Vibrant vegetarian and vegan recipes inspired by the flavours of East and Southeast Asia.
description: Meera Sodha grew up in a vegetarian household in Lincolnshire, where her mother cooked extraordinary Indian food every day. In East, she draws on her family's roots and her travels across Asia to deliver over 120 vegetarian and vegan recipes — from quick weeknight dals and stir-fries to elaborate celebration dishes. The book moves through Japan, China, Vietnam, Thailand, Indonesia, and the Indian subcontinent, offering everything from miso aubergine and peanut chaat to coconut laksa and cardamom rice pudding. It's a book about flavour without borders, about how vegetable cooking can be as rich and satisfying as any meat-based tradition.

title: Either/Or
author: Søren Kierkegaard
tagline: Kierkegaard's exploration of the aesthetic and ethical ways of living — through pseudonyms, seduction, and irony.
description: Written under multiple pseudonyms, Either/Or presents two ways of life: the aesthetic (devoted to pleasure, irony, and the moment) and the ethical (devoted to commitment, marriage, and duty). Kierkegaard does not resolve the tension — that is the point.

title: Emergent Strategy
author: adrienne maree brown
tagline: brown applies the principles of natural systems — fractals, adaptation, interdependence — to social justice organising.
description: What if movements for social change could learn from how mushrooms, starlings, and mycelium organise? brown proposes "emergent strategy" — small-scale pattern shifts that create large-scale transformation, grounded in biomimicry, decentralisation, and pleasure.

themeNotes.Ruins and Resilience: brown argues that the old models of top-down organising have failed — and that new forms of resilience must be learned from nature: adaptive, emergent, fractal. Change is not imposed but grown.

title: Empire: How Britain Made the Modern World
author: Niall Ferguson
tagline: A provocative history of the British Empire — its reach, contradictions, and legacy.
description: Ferguson argues that the British Empire, for all its brutality and exploitation, was a globalising force that spread free trade, the rule of law, parliamentary democracy, and the English language around the world. He traces the Empire from the pirates and sugar planters of the seventeenth century through the administrators and missionaries of the Victorian era to the soldiers of two world wars. The book is deliberately provocative — Ferguson acknowledges the Empire's crimes but insists on weighing them against its achievements. Whether you agree with his conclusions or not, the narrative is compulsively readable and the questions it raises about imperialism, globalisation, and responsibility remain urgent.

themeNotes.Seeing and Power: Ferguson tells the story of Empire from the coloniser's perspective — and that framing is itself an exercise of narrative power. Reading it alongside Said and Achebe reveals how the same history looks completely different depending on who controls the telling.

title: The End of Everything
author: Katie Mack
tagline: An astrophysicist walks through five ways the universe might end — with clarity, wit, and genuine wonder.
description: Heat death, the Big Crunch, the Big Rip, vacuum decay, and the Big Bounce — Mack explains each possible ending of the universe with scientific rigour and an infectious sense of awe at the cosmic scales involved.

title: Experimental Politics
author: Maurizio Lazzarato
tagline: Lazzarato on art, labour, and the politics of creativity in post-industrial capitalism.
description: Lazzarato argues that contemporary capitalism has captured creativity itself — making artistic labour the model for all work. He proposes an "experimental politics" that reclaims the power of invention from the market.

title: Falling Upward
author: Richard Rohr
tagline: Rohr on the two halves of life — the first builds the container, the second discovers the contents.
description: Rohr argues that the first half of life is about building identity, career, and ego — necessary work, but not the whole story. The second half begins when the container cracks: through failure, loss, or illness. Only then can the deeper self emerge.

themeNotes.Ruins and Resilience: Rohr reframes life's collapses — failure, loss, disillusionment — not as disasters but as necessary fallings that lead upward. Resilience is not about avoiding the fall but learning to fall well.

title: The Famished Road
author: Ben Okri
tagline: A spirit child navigates chaos and beauty in this Booker Prize-winning novel.
description: Azaro is an abiku — a spirit child caught between the world of the living and the world of the unborn, constantly tempted to return to the spirit realm. He chooses to stay in the living world, in a chaotic, unnamed Nigerian city on the brink of independence, where poverty, corruption, and beauty coexist in hallucinatory intensity. Okri creates a narrative that moves fluidly between the everyday and the mythic — political rallies blur into spirit carnivals, a boxing match becomes a cosmic battle. The novel is dense, overwhelming, and extraordinary — a work of African magical realism that creates its own category. Winner of the 1991 Booker Prize.

themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: Azaro sees the spirit world that exists beneath and alongside the physical one. Political corruption, poverty, and violence are shown to have a mythic dimension — the "famished road" of the title is a hunger that runs through reality itself.
themeNotes.Ruins and Resilience: Azaro chooses to stay in the living world despite its suffering. In a nation on the brink of independence, surrounded by chaos, the act of remaining — of refusing to return to the spirit realm — is itself a form of radical resilience.

title: The Fear of Freedom
author: Erich Fromm
tagline: Fromm asks why people flee from freedom into authoritarianism, conformity, and destructiveness.
description: Written in 1941 as fascism consumed Europe, Fromm argues that modern freedom — liberation from feudal bonds — created a psychic vacuum that people fill with submission to authority, compulsive conformity, or destructive rage. Still devastatingly relevant.

title: Film as a Subversive Art
author: Amos Vogel
tagline: A legendary survey of cinema's power to challenge taboos, politics, and the boundaries of perception.
description: Vogel — founder of Cinema 16, the most important film society in American history — surveys hundreds of films that push against censorship, convention, and the limits of what can be shown. From Buñuel to the avant-garde, from propaganda to pornography.

title: Finding the Mother Tree
author: Suzanne Simard
tagline: Simard's memoir of discovering the underground networks that connect forest trees.
description: The scientist who discovered that trees communicate and share nutrients through underground fungal networks — the "wood wide web" — tells her life story alongside her research. A book about what lies beneath forests, and about resilience in both ecological and personal forms.

themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: Simard's discovery is literal: beneath every forest lies a vast network of fungal connections through which trees share nutrients, send warnings, and nurture their young. The most important life in the forest is invisible.

title: Fischli Weiss: Flowers & Questions
author: Various (Tate)
tagline: The Tate retrospective of the Swiss art duo — from The Way Things Go to their polyurethane sculptures.
description: Peter Fischli and David Weiss spent three decades making art that oscillated between the banal and the sublime — rubber replicas of everyday objects, a feature-length film of chemical reactions, and photographs of airports and sunsets.

title: Five Centuries of British Painting
author: Andrew Wilson
tagline: A World of Art overview of British painting from Holbein to Hockney.
description: Wilson traces the development of a distinctly British painting tradition through portraiture, landscape, conversation pieces, the Pre-Raphaelites, and the School of London.

title: Flesh
author: David Szalay
tagline: Nine stories about men at different stages of life — desire, ageing, and the body's insistence.
description: Each story follows a man at a different age, from seventeen to seventy-three, as the body asserts itself through sex, illness, appetite, and decay. Szalay writes about masculinity with the clinical eye of a diagnostician.

title: Form and Object
author: Tristan Garcia
tagline: Garcia's systematic metaphysics — a philosophy of everything that begins with the simplest possible distinction.
description: Garcia starts from the minimal distinction between a thing and what that thing is in — between object and world — and builds from this into a complete metaphysics covering objects, events, classes, values, and the meaning of existence.

title: Free to Learn
author: Peter Gray
tagline: Gray argues that children learn best through free play — and that schools systematically destroy this capacity.
description: An evolutionary psychologist makes the case that the human instinct for play is the primary vehicle for learning, and that compulsory schooling undermines children's natural curiosity, creativity, and motivation.

title: Freud (Volume 2)
author: Sigmund Freud
tagline: Case studies and essays from the middle period of Freud's work — dreams, slips, and the mechanics of repression.
description: The second volume of Freud's collected works, covering the period when psychoanalysis was being formalised — including case studies, theoretical papers on the unconscious, and the development of the topographical model of the mind.

title: Sigmund Freud (Volume VIII)
author: Sigmund Freud
tagline: Freud's later theoretical works — beyond the pleasure principle, the ego and the id.
description: The eighth volume of Freud's collected works, encompassing his later metapsychological writings — the death drive, the structural model of the psyche, and the cultural criticism of Civilization and Its Discontents.

title: From Dawn to Decadence
author: Jacques Barzun
tagline: Barzun's sweeping history of 500 years of Western cultural life — from the Reformation to the present.
description: Written when Barzun was 93, this monumental work traces the ideas, movements, and personalities that shaped the modern West — from Luther and Erasmus through the Enlightenment and Romanticism to the twentieth century's crisis of confidence.

title: The Gay Science
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
tagline: Nietzsche's most joyful and personal work — "God is dead" appears here first.
description: Aphorisms, poems, and experiments in thought that range from the death of God to the eternal return, from the will to power to the "gay" (joyful) science of living without metaphysical comfort. The book where Nietzsche is most himself.

title: The God of Small Things
author: Arundhati Roy
tagline: A luminous story of forbidden love and the small things that shape lives in Kerala.
description: In the town of Ayemenem in Kerala, fraternal twins Rahel and Estha navigate the wreckage of their family — a divorce, a forbidden love affair, a death by drowning, and the rigid caste system that determines who can be loved and how. Roy's prose is extraordinary: lyrical, furious, and precise, moving between the children's perspective and the adults' world with devastating effect. The novel unfolds backwards, revealing its central tragedy slowly, so that when it arrives, the reader understands not just what happened but how the "Love Laws" — "the laws that lay down who should be loved, and how. And how much" — made it inevitable. Winner of the 1997 Booker Prize.

themeNotes.The Politics of Otherness: Roy's "Love Laws" — the unwritten rules of caste that determine who may be loved and how much — are the engine of this novel's tragedy. The Untouchable Velutha is destroyed not for what he does but for who he is.
themeNotes.The Body as Battleground: The forbidden relationship at the heart of the novel is punished through bodies — beaten, drowned, separated. Caste is enforced not through abstract rules but through the physical control of who may touch whom.

title: Selected Poetry
author: Johann Wolfgang von Goethe
tagline: Goethe's poetry in English translation — from the early Sturm und Drang lyrics to the late West-Eastern Divan.
description: Spanning six decades of writing, this selection reveals the range of Germany's greatest poet — erotic lyrics, philosophical ballads, nature poems, and the extraordinary late encounter with Persian poetry.

title: The Golden Mole
author: Catherine Rundell
tagline: Short essays on extraordinary animals — the narwhal, the swift, the golden mole — and why their survival matters.
description: Rundell writes about the animal kingdom with wonder and urgency, weaving natural history with personal reflection to argue that our fate is inseparable from the creatures we share the planet with.

title: Goya
author: Robert Hughes
tagline: Robert Hughes's brilliant biography of the Spanish master.
description: Robert Hughes traces Francisco Goya's extraordinary life from his early career as a court painter producing tapestries and royal portraits, through the illness that left him deaf, to the savage wartime etchings of The Disasters of War and the terrifying Black Paintings of his final years. Hughes — himself an art critic of fierce opinions — finds in Goya a kindred spirit: an artist who refused to look away from human cruelty and who used his art as a weapon against stupidity, violence, and superstition. This is art biography at its best — learned, passionate, and written with the clarity and force of great criticism.

themeNotes.Seeing and Power: Goya used his position as court painter to smuggle truth into images of power. His royal portraits are subtly unflattering; his war etchings are an act of witness against the state. Hughes shows an artist who understood that the act of seeing is never neutral.
themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: The Black Paintings of Goya's final years — Saturn devouring his son, witches' sabbaths, drowning dogs — reveal the nightmare beneath Enlightenment civilisation. Goya painted what polite society refused to see: the violence, madness, and cruelty that lie beneath the surface.

title: H is for Hawk
author: Helen Macdonald
tagline: Macdonald trains a goshawk while grieving her father — a memoir of wildness, loss, and the attempt to become something other than human.
description: After her father's sudden death, Macdonald buys a goshawk named Mabel and attempts to train her — a process that becomes an unravelling, a withdrawal from the human world into the hawk's alien consciousness. Woven through is the story of T.H. White's disastrous attempt to do the same.

themeNotes.Ruins and Resilience: Grief as a form of ruin — Macdonald's attempt to train a hawk is her attempt to survive the destruction of the world as she knew it. The hawk is both escape and means of return.

title: Harmony Korine
author: Darian Leader (intro)
tagline: A monograph on the filmmaker behind Kids, Gummo, and Spring Breakers.
description: An overview of Korine's transgressive cinema and visual art, with an introduction by psychoanalyst Darian Leader exploring the relationship between Korine's work and the aesthetics of the abject.

title: The Doctor's Kitchen
author: Dr. Rupy Aujla
tagline: An NHS doctor's guide to eating for health — over 100 recipes backed by nutritional science.
description: Dr. Aujla combines medical knowledge with practical recipes to show how food can be used to support health, reduce inflammation, and prevent chronic disease.

title: The Hermeneutics of the Subject
author: Michel Foucault
tagline: Foucault's lectures on the ancient practice of "care of the self" — how self-knowledge became self-discipline.
description: In these 1981-82 Collège de France lectures, Foucault traces how the ancient Greek injunction to "know yourself" was originally inseparable from practices of self-care — spiritual exercises, meditation, confession. He shows how Christianity and modernity stripped self-knowledge from self-care, creating the modern subject.

title: The Hero with a Thousand Faces
author: Joseph Campbell
tagline: Campbell's study of the monomyth — the universal pattern underlying all hero stories across cultures.
description: Campbell argues that the myths of every culture share a single underlying structure — the hero's journey: departure, initiation, return. Drawing on Freud, Jung, and worldwide mythology, he traces this pattern from the Buddha to Odysseus to Christ.

title: Hilma af Klint: Painting the Universe
author: Serpentine Galleries
tagline: The Serpentine exhibition catalogue for the Swedish artist who painted abstraction before Kandinsky.
description: Hilma af Klint was creating large-scale abstract paintings in 1906, years before Kandinsky's first abstractions. Working in secrecy, guided by séances and spiritual visions, she produced a body of work that challenges the entire narrative of modern art.

themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: Af Klint painted what she saw in séances and spiritual visions — the invisible forces beneath the material world. Her art makes the hidden visible, decades before the art world was ready to see it.

title: The Human Condition
author: Hannah Arendt
tagline: Arendt's analysis of labour, work, and action — the three fundamental activities that define human life on earth.
description: Arendt distinguishes between labour (the biological cycle of survival), work (the fabrication of a durable human world), and action (the capacity to begin something new in the public realm). She argues that modernity has elevated labour over action, reducing politics to economics and public life to mere survival.

themeNotes.Seeing and Power: Arendt argues that the public realm — the space of appearance where people see and are seen by others — is the foundation of political life. When this space is destroyed, power becomes invisible and unaccountable.

title: Hymns to the Goddess and Hymns to Kali
author: Arthur Avalon
tagline: Translations of Sanskrit hymns to the Divine Feminine — Kali, Durga, and the Goddess in her many forms.
description: Arthur Avalon (Sir John Woodroffe) translates sacred hymns from the Tantric tradition, presenting the Goddess as the dynamic, creative power of the universe — fierce, compassionate, and all-encompassing.

title: Ill Feelings
author: Alice Hattrick
tagline: A memoir of chronic illness shared between mother and daughter — when the body refuses to cooperate.
description: Hattrick writes about growing up with a mother who was chronically ill, and then becoming chronically ill herself. It's a book about invisible disability, about the medical establishment's failure to listen, and about the strange intimacy of shared suffering.

themeNotes.The Body as Battleground: Chronic illness makes the body a site of constant negotiation — with doctors who don't believe you, with a society that demands productivity, with your own expectations of what your body should be able to do.

title: Illuminations
author: Walter Benjamin
tagline: Benjamin's essential essays — on storytelling, mechanical reproduction, Kafka, and the angel of history.
description: This collection includes "The Work of Art in the Age of Mechanical Reproduction," "The Storyteller," and the "Theses on the Philosophy of History" with its unforgettable image of the angel of history blown backwards into the future by the storm of progress. Edited and introduced by Hannah Arendt.

themeNotes.Seeing and Power: Benjamin's essay on mechanical reproduction is the essential companion to Berger's Ways of Seeing — it asks what happens to art's "aura" when images can be infinitely copied.
themeNotes.Ruins and Resilience: The angel of history sees wreckage piled upon wreckage but cannot stop to mend it. Benjamin's image of progress as catastrophe is one of the most powerful in twentieth-century thought.

title: In Patagonia
author: Bruce Chatwin
tagline: Chatwin's genre-defining journey through southern Argentina.
description: Chatwin travelled to Patagonia in 1975, supposedly in search of a piece of brontosaurus skin that his grandmother had kept in a cabinet. What he found — or invented, or embroidered — became this strange, beautiful book: a collection of stories, encounters, and landscapes at the end of the world. He meets Welsh settlers, Anarchist outlaws, estancia owners, and wanderers. He visits the cave where the brontosaurus skin was found. He writes about Butch Cassidy, Charles Darwin, and the Patagonian giants. The book invented a genre — literary travel writing as a form of autobiography — and remains one of the most influential travel books ever written.

themeNotes.Ruins and Resilience: Patagonia as Chatwin finds it is a landscape of remnants — Welsh colonies that never quite took root, outlaw hideouts, extinct animals. The beauty of the book is in how life persists at the edge of the world, in the most inhospitable conditions.
themeNotes.Place and Belonging: Chatwin's Patagonia is half real, half invented — a place that exists as much in the imagination as on the map. The book asks whether belonging to a place requires living there, or whether some places belong to us through story and longing.

title: The Information Bomb
author: Paul Virilio
tagline: Virilio on the weaponisation of information and the dangers of real-time global communication.
description: Virilio argues that the "information bomb" — the instantaneous global transmission of data — is as destructive as the atomic bomb, eliminating the time needed for reflection, deliberation, and democratic decision-making.

title: Inner Experience
author: Georges Bataille
tagline: Bataille's account of ecstasy, limit-experience, and the dissolution of the self.
description: Bataille pursues experience to its extreme: laughter, eroticism, sacrifice, and the moment when the self dissolves. Inner Experience is both a philosophical text and a record of spiritual crisis — mysticism without God, ecstasy without salvation.

themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: Bataille seeks what lies beneath rational thought: ecstasy, terror, the sacred. Inner experience is the eruption of the unknown through the crust of the everyday.
themeNotes.The Body as Battleground: For Bataille, limit-experience happens in the body — through pain, pleasure, and the dissolution of boundaries. The body is where the self comes undone.

title: Interaction of Color
author: Josef Albers
tagline: Albers's masterclass in colour perception — how colours change depending on their neighbours.
description: Based on Albers's legendary course at Yale, this book demonstrates through practical exercises that colour is the most relative medium in art — the same colour looks completely different depending on what surrounds it. A foundational text for anyone who works with colour.

themeNotes.Seeing and Power: Albers proves that we never see colour objectively — context determines everything. A lesson in how what we see is always shaped by what surrounds it.

title: The Interface Effect
author: Alexander Galloway
tagline: Galloway on how interfaces — screens, windows, doorways — structure our relationship to power and information.
description: Galloway argues that the interface is not a transparent window onto data but an active agent that shapes what can be seen, known, and done. Drawing on media theory and philosophy, he develops a politics of the interface.

title: The Interior Castle
author: St. Teresa of Ávila
tagline: Teresa's account of the soul's journey through seven mansions toward union with God.
description: Written in 1577, The Interior Castle describes the soul as a crystal castle with seven concentric dwellings. The mystic moves inward through prayer, self-knowledge, and suffering until reaching the innermost mansion where the soul unites with God. One of the great texts of Christian mysticism.

themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: Teresa maps the hidden architecture of the soul — seven mansions beneath the surface of ordinary consciousness, each deeper and more luminous than the last. The ultimate journey inward.

title: Interrogating the Real
author: Slavoj Žižek
tagline: A selection of Žižek's key essays — on ideology, Lacan, popular culture, and the nature of the Real.
description: Žižek at his most accessible and provocative, reading Hitchcock through Lacan, dismantling liberal ideology, and arguing that what we call "reality" is structured by fantasies we refuse to examine.

title: The Invention of Tradition
author: Eric Hobsbawm & Terence Ranger
tagline: How traditions that appear ancient are often quite recent inventions.
description: This collection of essays demonstrates that many "traditions" we assume to be ancient were actually invented relatively recently — often in the nineteenth century — to serve political purposes. The Highland tradition of Scotland (including tartan and clan identity), the British monarchy's elaborate ceremonies, and colonial rituals in Africa are all shown to be deliberate constructions designed to establish continuity with a suitable past and to legitimate present authority. Hobsbawm's introduction provides the theoretical framework, and the case studies — by historians including Hugh Trevor-Roper and Terence Ranger — are consistently surprising and illuminating.

themeNotes.Seeing and Power: Hobsbawm reveals that what appears to be timeless tradition is often recent fabrication designed to legitimate power. The kilt, the royal ceremony, the colonial ritual — all invented to create the illusion of ancient authority. Seeing through the invention is an act of intellectual liberation.

title: A Short History of Islamic Thought
author: Fitzroy Morrissey
tagline: A lucid survey of fourteen centuries of Islamic intellectual life — theology, philosophy, mysticism, and law.
description: Morrissey traces the development of Islamic thought from the Prophet Muhammad to the present, covering the great thinkers — al-Ghazali, Ibn Rushd, Rumi, Ibn Khaldun — and the living traditions of Sunni, Shia, and Sufi thought.

title: Jerusalem
author: Yotam Ottolenghi & Sami Tamimi
tagline: A celebration of the food of Jerusalem, shaped by the cultures that converge there.
description: Two chefs — one Israeli, one Palestinian — both born in Jerusalem in the same year, collaborate on a book about the food of their shared, divided city. Jerusalem explores the culinary traditions of the Arab, Jewish, Christian, and Armenian communities, from the hummus stalls of the Old City to the spice markets and home kitchens. The recipes — roasted aubergine with tahini and pomegranate, lamb shawarma, chopped salads, stuffed vegetables — are deeply rooted in the city's layered history. It's a book about what food can hold when politics cannot.

title: King Kong Theory
author: Virginie Despentes
tagline: Despentes writes about rape, prostitution, and pornography with the fury of someone who has lived through all three.
description: Part memoir, part manifesto, Despentes refuses every polite feminist position and writes from the gutter — about being raped, about working in pornography, about the anger that respectable feminism would rather not acknowledge. Raw, confrontational, and essential.

title: Kyoto: A Cultural and Culinary Guide
author: Various
tagline: An exploration of Kyoto through its food, temples, and traditions.
description: A guide to Kyoto that goes beyond the tourist trail, exploring the ancient capital through its food culture, tea ceremonies, temple gardens, and seasonal rituals. The book traces the development of kaiseki cuisine — the multi-course meal that elevates simplicity to high art — and visits the tofu shops, pickle makers, and market stalls that have sustained the city for centuries. It's a meditation on how a place can preserve its traditions while remaining alive, and on the Japanese aesthetic of finding beauty in impermanence.

title: Là-Bas
author: J.K. Huysmans
tagline: Huysmans's novel of Satanism in fin-de-siècle Paris — black masses, occultism, and the longing for the supernatural.
description: A writer researching the life of the medieval child-murderer Gilles de Rais is drawn into the world of contemporary Parisian Satanism. Huysmans writes as someone genuinely drawn to the supernatural — not debunking it but exploring its pull.

title: Labyrinths
author: Jorge Luis Borges
tagline: The essential Borges — stories and essays that turn reality into an infinite library.
description: Borges creates worlds where libraries contain every possible book, where a map becomes as large as the territory it represents, where a man dreams another man into existence. These stories — "The Library of Babel," "The Garden of Forking Paths," "Pierre Menard, Author of the Quixote" — changed what fiction could do.

themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: Borges reveals infinite structures beneath the surface of reality — libraries that contain all possible books, gardens where time forks into parallel futures. His fiction makes the hidden architecture of the world visible.

title: The Seminar: The Formations of the Unconscious
author: Jacques Lacan
tagline: Lacan's 1957–58 seminar on jokes, the unconscious, and the structure of desire.
description: Lacan reads Freud's Jokes and Their Relation to the Unconscious through structuralist linguistics, developing his theory that the unconscious is structured like a language. The seminar also introduces the graph of desire.

themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: Lacan's entire project is an investigation of what lies beneath — the unconscious structured like a language, operating beneath conscious speech, shaping desire without the subject's knowledge.

title: The Lacanian Subject
author: Bruce Fink
tagline: The clearest introduction to Lacan's theory of the subject — for those who've tried and failed to read Lacan directly.
description: Fink translates Lacan's notoriously difficult concepts — the Other, jouissance, the Real, the divided subject — into accessible prose, making the case that Lacanian psychoanalysis offers a fundamentally different understanding of human experience.

title: The Language of Things
author: Deyan Sudjic
tagline: Sudjic on how designed objects communicate — status, identity, desire, and power.
description: The director of the Design Museum explores how objects speak to us — from luxury goods to military hardware to Apple products — and how design shapes the way we understand ourselves and our world.

title: Les Misérables
author: Victor Hugo
tagline: Hugo's towering epic of justice, mercy, and redemption in post-revolutionary France.
description: Jean Valjean, released after nineteen years in prison for stealing a loaf of bread, breaks his parole and remakes his life — as a factory owner, a mayor, and the guardian of the orphaned Cosette. Pursued relentlessly by the inspector Javert, who believes in the law above all else, Valjean's story becomes an epic meditation on justice, mercy, and what it means to be good in an unjust society. Hugo weaves in the Paris sewers, the Battle of Waterloo, street urchins, revolutionaries, and the 1832 June Rebellion. It's sprawling, digressive, and magnificent — a novel that contains an entire civilisation.

themeNotes.The Politics of Otherness: Valjean is branded an ex-convict and permanently excluded from society for stealing bread. Hugo shows how poverty itself is a form of othering — the poor are not citizens but threats to be managed.
themeNotes.Ruins and Resilience: The novel is built around reinvention after destruction — Valjean rebuilds his life from nothing, Paris rebuilds after revolution, and Hugo argues that mercy, not law, is what allows human beings to survive their own history.

title: Letters to a Young Poet
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
tagline: Rilke's advice to a young writer — on solitude, patience, and living the questions.
description: Ten letters written between 1903 and 1908 to a young aspiring poet. Rilke counsels patience, solitude, and the willingness to live within uncertainty: "Be patient toward all that is unsolved in your heart and try to love the questions themselves."

title: Letting Go
author: David R. Hawkins
tagline: Hawkins's map of consciousness and a practical method for releasing suppressed emotions.
description: Hawkins proposes a simple technique: instead of resisting, expressing, or projecting emotions, simply let them be — observe them without judgement and allow them to pass. The book maps emotional states from shame to enlightenment on a numerical scale.

title: A Life's Work: On Becoming a Mother
author: Rachel Cusk
tagline: An unflinching account of early motherhood — its demands and its strange new clarity.
description: Cusk writes about the experience of having her first child with the same unflinching honesty she brings to her fiction. She describes the physical shock of birth, the obliteration of the self that follows, the boredom and terror and unexpected tenderness of caring for a newborn. She's unsparing about the losses — of freedom, of identity, of the person she was before — and precise about the gains, which are harder to name. The book caused controversy when it was published, accused of being "anti-motherhood," but it's really the opposite: it takes motherhood seriously enough to tell the truth about it.

themeNotes.The Body as Battleground: Motherhood as Cusk describes it is an overwhelmingly physical experience — the body split open by birth, colonised by a newborn's needs, reshaped beyond recognition. The self doesn't just change; it is obliterated and rebuilt around the body's new demands.

title: Living in the End Times
author: Slavoj Žižek
tagline: Žižek reads the global crisis through denial, anger, bargaining, depression, and acceptance.
description: Structured around the Kübler-Ross stages of grief, Žižek argues that global capitalism is approaching its terminal crisis and maps our collective responses — from ideological denial to revolutionary possibility.

title: Locality, Regeneration & Diversities
author: Bennett & Butler
tagline: Essays on how communities regenerate through diversity — the politics of place-making.
description: A collection examining how urban regeneration intersects with cultural diversity, community identity, and the politics of belonging in contemporary cities.

title: Londoners: The Days and Nights of London Now
author: Craig Taylor
tagline: An oral history of London told through the voices of its inhabitants.
description: Craig Taylor spent five years interviewing Londoners — over two hundred people from every corner of the city. The result is a polyphonic portrait assembled from the voices of black-cab drivers, hedge-fund managers, refugees, sex workers, lollipop ladies, and tube workers. They talk about arriving in London for the first time, about loneliness and belonging, about the smell of the city and the weight of its history. It's a book that captures the lived reality of one of the world's great cities — not the tourist London but the London of daily life, endlessly various and often profoundly moving.

themeNotes.The Politics of Otherness: The voices in this book include refugees, immigrants, and people who feel like strangers in their own city. Taylor lets them speak for themselves, revealing how belonging in London is never settled — it's always being questioned, claimed, and contested.
themeNotes.Place and Belonging: London emerges not as a fixed place but as two hundred different cities layered on top of each other. Each voice adds another London. The book argues that a city is nothing more — or less — than the sum of the lives lived in it.

title: The Lost Spirit of Capitalism
author: Bernard Stiegler
tagline: Stiegler diagnoses the exhaustion of consumer capitalism and argues for a new political economy of contribution.
description: Building on Max Weber's thesis about capitalism's "spirit," Stiegler argues that this spirit has been destroyed by capitalism itself — replaced by drive-based consumption that produces addiction, depression, and ecological collapse.

title: Malicious Damage
author: Ilsa Colsell
tagline: The story of the punk art movement and its collision with the British establishment.
description: Colsell documents the art that emerged from punk — from Jamie Reid's Sex Pistols graphics to the broader culture of DIY aesthetics, zines, and deliberate ugliness that challenged the art world's assumptions about beauty and value.

title: A Man's Place
author: Annie Ernaux
tagline: Ernaux reconstructs her father's life — working-class, barely educated, proud and ashamed in equal measure.
description: After her father's death, Ernaux writes his biography in flat, affectless prose — deliberately refusing the literary flourishes that would betray the distance between her educated self and his working-class world. A masterpiece of auto-sociobiography.

themeNotes.The Politics of Otherness: Ernaux writes about class as a form of othering that operates within families. Her education separated her from her father's world — she became other to her own origins.

title: Matter and Memory
author: Henri Bergson
tagline: Bergson's radical argument that memory is not stored in the brain but persists in time itself.
description: Bergson dismantles the assumption that the brain is a container for memories, arguing instead that the past survives in its entirety and that perception is always shaped by the virtual pressure of memory. A key influence on Deleuze and on process philosophy more broadly.

themeNotes.What Lies Beneath: For Bergson, the entire past lies beneath every moment of perception — not stored somewhere, but coexisting with the present. Memory is what lies beneath consciousness, shaping everything we see.

title: The Meaning of Things
author: A.C. Grayling
tagline: Short philosophical essays on the concepts that shape everyday life — from courage to death to love.
description: Grayling writes accessible, elegant essays on the ideas we live by — beauty, ambition, sorrow, hope — drawing on philosophy, literature, and history to illuminate what these concepts actually mean in practice.

title: Meeting the Universe Halfway
author: Karen Barad
tagline: Barad's "agential realism" — matter is not passive stuff but an active participant in the world's becoming.
description: Drawing on quantum physics and feminist theory, Barad argues that the boundaries between observer and observed, human and non-human, are not given but enacted through "intra-actions." A philosophically demanding rethinking of what matter, meaning, and measurement are.

title: Melancholy I–II
author: Jon Fosse
tagline: Fosse reimagines the life of the Norwegian painter Lars Hertervig — madness, art, and the borders of perception.
description: Based on the life of the nineteenth-century painter Lars Hertervig, who was committed to an asylum, Fosse writes in his characteristic hypnotic, repetitive prose — circling obsessively around moments of beauty and collapse. Art and madness become indistinguishable.

title: Minima Moralia
author: Theodor W. Adorno
tagline: Reflections from a damaged life — aphorisms on culture, morality, and exile written during World War II.
description: Written in American exile during the war, these 153 aphorisms dissect the corruptions of everyday life under capitalism — from the decline of gift-giving to the impossibility of dwelling. "There is no right life in the wrong one" is its most famous line.

themeNotes.Ruins and Resilience: Written from the ruins of European civilisation, Minima Moralia asks whether any form of good life is possible in a damaged world. Adorno's answer is bleak but his persistence in asking is itself a form of intellectual resilience.

title: The Moosewood Cookbook
author: Mollie Katzen
tagline: The hand-lettered classic that brought vegetarian cooking into the mainstream.
description: Originally published in 1977 and hand-lettered by Katzen herself, The Moosewood Cookbook emerged from the collectively run Moosewood Restaurant in Ithaca, New York, and became one of the best-selling cookbooks of all time. The recipes — mushroom-barley soup, spanakopita, tahini dressing, baba ganoush — now feel like old friends, but at the time they were revolutionary: proof that meatless food could be hearty, satisfying, and full of flavour. The charming hand-drawn illustrations and handwritten recipes give it a warmth that no digital cookbook has matched.

title: Morpho: Skeleton and Bone Reference Points
author: Michel Lauricella
tagline: An artist's reference guide to the human skeleton — bones as the architecture of the body.
description: Lauricella provides detailed drawings of every bone in the human body from multiple angles, with annotations showing how skeletal structure determines the surface forms that artists draw and sculpt.

title: Mothers: An Essay on Love and Cruelty
author: Jacqueline Rose
tagline: Rose argues that motherhood is the place where society deposits its most impossible demands.
description: Rose examines how mothers are simultaneously idealised and blamed — made responsible for everything that goes wrong while denied the resources to do anything right. She draws on psychoanalysis, literature, and current affairs to argue that our hatred of mothers reveals something fundamental about how societies manage their own cruelty.

themeNotes.The Body as Battleground: Rose shows how the mother's body becomes a site of social control — expected to sacrifice, nurture, and absorb blame. Motherhood is the battleground where society's cruelest contradictions are played out.
themeNotes.The Politics of Otherness: Mothers are simultaneously central to society and othered by it — idealised in the abstract, punished in practice. Rose reveals the politics behind this paradox.

title: The Mushroom at the End of the World
author: Anna Lowenhaupt Tsing
tagline: Following the matsutake mushroom through ruined landscapes.
description: The matsutake mushroom is one of the most valuable fungi in the world, prized in Japan and found growing in the ruins of industrial forests.
